bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the case as emblematic of Trump-world corruption, leaning on terms like “corrupt,” “scandal,” and “insider trading” to suggest systemic abuse and to widen the story beyond one aide; right-leaning coverage is similarly sharp but more tabloid-like, using words such as “disgrace,” “caught,” and “shame” to spotlight personal wrongdoing and embarrassment.
Center-Leaning pieces stay more procedural, stressing that the operator was “accused” and that regulators are investigating a “first known instance” of potential abuse.
All sides highlight the roughly $100,000 profit and the teleprompter role, but they diverge on whether this is a market vulnerability, a White House scandal, or a regulatory case.
summary: A longtime White House teleprompter operator has been placed on unpaid leave following federal allegations that he used advance knowledge of President Trump's speeches to make over $100,000 on online prediction markets.
Gabriel Perez, who has managed Trump's teleprompter since 2016, is under investigation by the Commodity Futures Trading Commission for utilizing draft speeches to place wagers on the platform Kalshi.

The bets targeted Kalshi’s "Mentions" market, where users wager on whether a public speaker will use specific words, phrases, or topics. Perez allegedly bet on more than a dozen addresses, including the State of the Union, a Davos summit speech, and a Medal of Honor ceremony.
Perez reportedly monitored and adjusted his bets in real time during live broadcasts, sometimes exiting positions early when Trump deviated from the prepared teleprompter text to go off-script.

The activity was flagged by Kalshi's internal surveillance team, which subsequently froze his account, secured roughly $90,000 to $100,000 in trading profits, and referred the case to federal regulators.
title: Source Icon

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the address as another episode of election denial, using terms like “fact-checked,” “baseless,” and “stirring misinformation” to stress Trump’s claim was unsupported and politically motivated; it often links his remarks to broader efforts for stricter voting laws.

Right-Leaning coverage, by contrast, treats the same speech as an exposure of hidden danger, leaning on phrases like “election integrity,” “shocking vulnerabilities,” “deep state,” and “censorship” to cast the release as a breakthrough and media refusal to air it as bias.
Both sides focus on the 220 million voter-file claim, but they diverge sharply on whether it signals fraud or fearmongering.
summary: On Thursday, July 16, 2026, President Donald Trump delivered a primetime address declassifying documents alleging China illicitly acquired 220 million U.S. voter files and compromised election infrastructure.
Trump's accusations contradict a March 2021 U.S. intelligence assessment that found no evidence foreign actors altered 2020 election results, including voter registrations, ballots, or tabulation.
Trump urged Congress to pass the SAVE America Act, which mandates photo identification and proof of citizenship for voter registration, framing it as essential election security.
Senate Democratic leader Chuck Schumer criticized the address, while ABC and NBC declined to broadcast it live on their primary networks, opting for streaming platforms instead.
Experts noted the address repackaged old claims as President Trump's approval rating sat at 37%, complicating Republican efforts to defend congressional majorities ahead of November midterms.
title: Trump Alleges Chinese Election Meddling in Primetime Address

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the tariff as a politically charged escalation, using loaded terms like “hits,” “launches,” and “what’s ahead” to stress conflict, skepticism, and Trump’s broader trade agenda, while right-leaning coverage portrays it as a justified response to “unfair trade practices,” often sharpening the tone with words like “slaps,” “steep,” and “socialist-run” to cast Brazil as ideologically suspect.

The biggest dividing lines are whether the move is seen as retaliation or as leverage against censorship and bad faith, and how Brazil’s reciprocity threat is read: as escalation or proof of U.S. resolve.
Both sides note the 25% tariff, July 22 start date, and exemptions like beef and coffee, but they place them in sharply different ideological frames.
summary: The United States is imposing 25% tariffs on some imports from Brazil starting July 22 due to unfair trade practices by Brazil, the world's 10th-largest economy.
Senior Trump administration officials confirmed the tariffs, first proposed last month, will begin on July 22, with some goods exempted to avoid disrupting supply chains.

The U.S. Office of the Trade Representative found Brazil engaged in unfair trade practices, including lax anti-corruption enforcement and imposing unfair tariffs, making the tariffs necessary to level the playing field.
title: US Announces New 25% Tariffs on Brazil for ‘Unfair’ Trade Practices

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames Trump’s move as a troubling contradiction, using sharper words like “undercutting,” “rejects,” and “not change a thing” to spotlight harm, accountability, and the “third killing” or broader fatality pattern; right-leaning coverage instead casts it as a decisive restoration of enforcement, with phrases like “reverses,” “resume,” “stand-down,” and “on notice” signaling urgency and suspicion toward the pause.

The key dividing line is whether ICE traffic stops are portrayed as a dangerous escalation or a “crime-fighting tool,” with right-side emphasis on “illegal immigrants” and left-side emphasis on public fear and violence.
Both sides agree Trump overrode the pause, but they read that clash through starkly different ideological lenses.
summary: On Wednesday, July 15, 2026, President Donald Trump directed Immigration and Customs Enforcement to resume traffic stops, overturning a temporary suspension the Department of Homeland Security had imposed following two fatal shootings.

The suspension followed fatal shootings of 52-year-old Lorenzo Salgado Araujo in Houston on July 7 and 25-year-old Johan Sebastian Duran Guerrero in Biddeford, Maine, on July 13, both during ICE operations.
Agency officials acknowledged neither Salgado Araujo nor Guerrero was the intended target of the operations; agents lacked body cameras during both encounters, fueling demands for accountability.
White House border czar Tom Homan initially described the pause as a short-term review, but President Trump's public intervention forced a swift reversal less than 24 hours later.

Lawmakers including Maine Senator Susan Collins are demanding independent investigations into ICE traffic stops, a tactic linked to at least 10 deaths since the mass deportation campaign began in January 2025.
title: Trump says ICE won't halt traffic stops after recent fatal shootings

bias: Left coverage frames the story as a tragic, evolving accident, stressing shifting casualty counts, the memorial/ash-scattering context, and later corrections like “no evidence” of fire; right coverage turns the same event into a more dramatic “massive” or “terrifying” rescue narrative, leaning on words like “DEVELOPING,” “massive,” and “fire” to heighten urgency and preserve the disaster angle.
Center pieces sit between them, staying with official counts and the rescue operation.
Both sides agree on the Alcatraz setting, the pontoon boat, and the Coast Guard response, but diverge over whether the key meaning is careful updating or crisis spectacle.
summary: One person died and two others were missing after a triple-deck pontoon boat with 19 people capsized near Alcatraz Island in San Francisco Bay, according to officials.
San Francisco Fire Chief Dean Crispen said that 11 vessels were searching for the two missing people and that the search would continue for hours.
Contrary to early reports, there was no fire on board the boat, Fire Chief Dean Crispen stated.
Of the 19 people on board, 13 were safely on shore, three were hospitalized, one died, and two remained missing, as reported by Fire Chief Dean Crispen.
title: 1 missing and 17 rescued after a boat fire near Alcatraz Island off San Francisco

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the vote as a practical fix to a “pesky” or “ditch the switch” annoyance, while also spotlighting health experts who call permanent DST the “wrong idea” and warn of circadian-rhythm risks; right-leaning coverage instead casts it as an “important step” and a win for Trump, using phrases like “Trump-backed,” “outdated practice,” and “landslide” to signal momentum and political validation.
The key dividing lines are Trump’s role, the medical-safety debate, and whether later sunsets are a benefit or darker winter mornings a problem.
Both sides agree on the 308-117 vote and Senate next steps, but they place the broader cultural stakes in very different frames.
summary: On Tuesday, the House voted 308-117 to pass the Sunshine Protection Act, advancing legislation backed by President Donald Trump that would allow states to make Daylight Saving Time permanent.
Rep. Vern Buchanan, R-Fla., who authored the bill, said Americans are "tired of the biannual time change," while Nineteen states have already passed legislation to make the change permanent.
House Energy and Commerce ranking member Rep. Frank Pallone, D-N.J., told lawmakers that "the benefits of stopping the biannual changing of the clocks are numerous and clear," citing tourism boosts.
Sen. Tom Cotton, R-Ark., warned the act would result in "absurdly late" winter sunrises, while the American Medical Association argues year-round standard time better aligns with natural daylight.
The Senate must now pass the bill, which requires at least 60 votes to succeed, though a 1974 attempt at permanent daylight saving time was repealed less than a year later.
title: House passes bill that would make daylight saving time permanent

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames Xi’s speech as a push for a fairer global AI order, stressing “equitable access,” support for developing countries, and criticism of U.S. export controls and “overstretching” security fears; right-leaning coverage recasts the same remarks as geopolitical brinkmanship, using sharper cues like “seemingly targeting the U.S.,” “secure, controllable,” and “solo performance” to spotlight rivalry and suspicion.
The pivotal divide is whether the conference is a cooperation story or a power contest, even though both sides agree Xi is challenging single-country dominance and elevating China ’s AI role.
summary: On Friday, Chinese President Xi Jinping used the World Artificial Intelligence Conference in Shanghai to position Beijing as a champion of a new global AI order, promoting open-source technology while challenging Washington's influence over the sector.

Xi's speech followed the establishment of the China-created World AI Cooperation Organisation, which signed up 29 member countries on Thursday, marking a "milestone in the history of world AI development" and responding to Global South demands for participation.

Alongside China's leading technology companies, Beijing-based startup Moonshot AI unveiled Kimi K3 on Friday, claiming the world's largest open AI model by parameter count, as Xi urged countries to "seize the rare and historic opportunity" of open-source AI.

The July 17-20 gathering elevates WAIC into a test of China's influence as Beijing and Washington prepare for their first government-level AI talks under President Donald Trump's administration, contrasting their competing visions for global rules.

China's coalition acts as a rival to the Washington-led "Pax Silica" initiative, while Xi warned of "new historical injustices" from unequal access to technology and urged measures to guard against loss-of-control scenarios.
title: Xi pitches China as leader of new global AI order, challenging US dominance

summary: On Friday, Chevron Corp. is set to sign memorandums of understanding with the Iraqi government to advance the U.S. oil major's potential entry into the West Qurna-2 and Nassiriya oilfields.
Iraq seeks to reduce reliance on the Persian Gulf chokepoint after the Strait of Hormuz blockade forced the country to cut oil production by more than 60% during the war.
Evaluating the revival of the long-defunct 500-mile Kirkuk-Baniyas pipeline, Chevron is exploring projects the U.S. administration supports to link Iraq's oilfields to the Mediterranean Sea.
Iraqi Prime Minister Ali Al-Zaidi visited Chevron's Houston headquarters on Thursday following his Washington meetings with President Donald Trump earlier this week to discuss energy partnerships.
Although talks have spanned 12 to 18 months, Chevron cautioned that final agreements remain a 'long ways from the finish line,' signaling lengthy negotiations ahead.
title: Chevron to sign MOUs with Iraq for West Qurna 2 and Nassiriya oilfields

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames Jeffries’ speech as a substantive campaign pitch, highlighting affordability, voting rights, and a Democratic House majority advancing “progressive policies,” while the right-leaning excerpt narrows the story to political combat and union strategy, stressing “hammers Trump” and “teachers union” help for the midterms.

The left uses energizing coalition language like “rallied a key group,” pairing it with policy substance; the right’s terser, snippet-like tone de-emphasized those details, amplifying confrontation and insider maneuvering.
The key dividing line is whether the speech is read as issue-driven mobilization or partisan attack, though both agree it was aimed at securing union support for November.
summary: On Thursday, House Democratic Leader Hakeem Jeffries addressed the American Federation of Teachers national convention, urging the 1.8 million-member union to help Democrats win enough House races in November to secure a majority next year.

Weingarten criticized the House Committee on Education and the Workforce on Wednesday for approving 10 bills to dismantle the Department of Education, arguing the committee prioritizes fighting unions over helping students.

The AFT remains a major benefactor to Democrats, contributing more than $1.9 million to candidates and $2.1 million to the House Majority PAC during the 2024 election cycle, according to Open Secrets.

Jeffries pledged that Democrats would fight to "clean up corruption" within President Donald Trump's administration and rein in ICE initiatives, which he called "completely and totally out of control."

Georgia Democrat Raphael Warnock also spoke at the convention, emphasizing that public education and Pell grants enabled his rise to the Senate while warning many children today lack such opportunities.
title: Hakeem Jeffries Urges AFT to Help Democrats Win House Majority

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the story as a spiraling war, stressing “fresh wave,” “little sign of de-escalating,” and even “torpedoing diplomatic efforts for peace,” so the central dividing line is whether U.S. strikes are seen as dangerous escalation or as justified pressure.
Right-Leaning coverage is more hard-edged and security-driven, using terms like “attacks,” “bombard,” and “poses a threat” to cast Iran as a maritime danger and the U.S. response as necessary.
Center coverage stays more descriptive, adding casualty figures and spillover risks like the Houthis and Red Sea shipping.
All sides agree on rising hostilities, but differ sharply on blame, urgency, and regional stakes.
summary: On Friday, Iran launched fresh attacks on U.S. facilities in the Gulf after a sixth consecutive night of U.S. strikes on Iranian military facilities, as last month's truce descended into daily exchanges of fire.
The escalation stems from Iran's intent to enforce passage fees for ships using the Strait of Hormuz at the end of a 60-day negotiation period established in last month's memorandum of understanding.

U.S. Central Command completed strikes to "further degrade Iranian military capabilities," including on Qeshm Island and near Bandar Abbas; state news agency IRNA reported seven people died in attacks on bridges in Bandar Khamir.

White House press secretary Karoline Leavitt said Thursday that President Donald Trump would not "sit by and allow these active acts of terrorism to take place in the strait," though he remains open to diplomacy.

Iran could prod Houthi allies in Yemen to close the Bab al-Mandeb strait if Washington continues attacking its infrastructure , sources told Reuters, as residents in Tehran expressed exhaustion over the renewed conflict.
title: Iran launches fresh attacks after sixth day of US strikes

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the strikes as a humanitarian and legal crisis, stressing “civilian infrastructure ,” “barbaric” conduct, and even “war crimes,” especially when hospitals and children are near the blast zone; right-leaning coverage recasts the same attacks as hard-nosed pressure, using terms like “IRGC stronghold,” “choke off,” and “supply routes” to justify escalation against a hostile regime.

The dividing line is whether bridge strikes are portrayed as coercive brutality or strategic disruption, with casualty figures and the collapse of a tower at Chabahar used by both sides but read through opposite lenses.
Center coverage stays more restrained, focusing on what was hit and the widening campaign.
summary: The United States expanded its military campaign against Iran with a sixth consecutive night of airstrikes, shifting its strategy to target critical infrastructure.
Overnight strikes in Iran's southern Hormozgan province hit several bridges, including in the coastal city of Bandar Khamir, resulting in at least seven deaths.
The U.S. bombardment also targeted Chabahar port on the Gulf of Oman, apparently collapsing a key maritime traffic control tower at the strategic trade gateway.
In immediate retaliation for the infrastructure damage, Iran launched fresh missile barrages toward U.S.-allied regional partners, triggering air defenses and warning sirens in Qatar.
President Donald Trump defended the escalation in a primetime address, asserting that the campaign is succeeding and aiming to force Iran to ease its blockade on the Strait of Hormuz.
title: US strikes bridges and collapses a tower at a key port as its Iran campaign expands

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the finding as a breakthrough for alien life, using upbeat phrases like “best place to look for life,” “major milestone,” and even “aliens” to amplify discovery and possibility, while also noting the 2024 helium signal versus 2025 non-detection to stress changing conditions.

Right-Leaning coverage is similarly celebratory but more assertive, leaning on “enormous milestone,” “authentic giant step,” and “all the conditions for life,” often adding institutional authority and layered-atmosphere detail.
The sharpest divide is caution versus certainty: the left highlights evolving uncertainty, the right foregrounds life-friendly promise.
Both agree the “first atmosphere” on a rocky habitable-zone world is a major step in the broader search for life beyond Earth.
summary: On Thursday, July 16, 2026, astronomers published a study in Science confirming the first detection of an atmosphere on a rocky exoplanet named LHS 1140 b, which orbits within its star's habitable zone.

Despite the violent reputations of M dwarf stars, which often strip atmospheres from planets, this 'super-Earth' located 49 light-years away has retained a helium-dominated atmosphere for more than 3 billion years.
Lead author Collin Cherubim of Harvard University predicted the helium signature using a computer model, which the team validated by observing the planet with Magellan Telescopes in Chile.
The detection positions LHS 1140 b as a potential 'helium world' with liquid water, though Cherubim cautioned the atmosphere is likely very different from Earth's.
This milestone provides the strongest evidence yet that rocky, habitable-zone worlds can maintain protective atmospheres, offering a critical new target for astrobiology and the search for life.
title: First atmosphere detected on a habitable Earth-like planet

summary: Senator Eric Schmitt urged the State Department and Homeland Security to investigate illegal citizenship grants to children of foreign diplomats, stating that such citizenship is not supported by the Constitution or federal law and poses national security risks.

Public reports suggest that thousands of children of foreign diplomats may have received U.S. citizenship documents despite being ineligible, and Schmitt called for policies to prevent this and to revoke unlawfully obtained citizenship.
The Biden administration has not updated diplomatic staff lists, complicating the identification of diplomats' children when government agencies issue birth certificates and Social Security numbers.

Schmitt introduced the American Citizenship Act aiming to limit birthright citizenship to children of U.S. citizens and lawful permanent residents, following challenges to Supreme Court rulings on citizenship for children of certain noncitizens.
title: Sen. Eric Schmitt Introduces Bill Targeting Birth Tourism and Diplomatic Birthright Citizenship

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the scene as a morally charged abuse of power, using words like “outrage,” “tackle,” and “elderly man” to spotlight harm and vulnerability, while right-leaning coverage shifts the same event toward order-and-disruption language, leaning on phrases like “attempted detention” and “disturbance” to foreground police response and public disorder.

The key dividing line is whether ICE’s conduct or the enforcement context drives the story: the center adds the later LAX arrest and the claim of a visa overstay, but left and right de-emphasize that follow-through in different ways.

Both sides agree the airport encounter went viral and was interrupted by bystanders, yet they diverge sharply on whether it signals misconduct or an enforcement episode amid broader immigration tensions.
summary: Immigration and Customs Enforcement officers tried to detain a man at Las Vegas airport, but bystanders intervened, causing the officers to abandon the arrest.
The man, Phu Nguyen of Vietnam who had overstayed his visa, was later arrested at Los Angeles airport.
A video captured the scene, showing plainclothes officers being confronted by a crowd before walking away.
title: Outrage after ICE agents tackle man at Las Vegas airport

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the $95 billion package as a “long-shot effort,” stressing Republicans “steamrolling” Democrats and using charged terms like “war package” to foreground the Iran conflict and political brinkmanship.

Right-Leaning coverage instead emphasizes GOP momentum and conservative unease, with words like “survives,” “critical hurdle,” and “fiscal hawk revolt,” while also spotlighting deficit risk and the fact it doesn’t defund Planned Parenthood—an issue largely de-emphasized elsewhere.

Both sides agree the committee advanced the plan on a party-line vote and faces steep hurdles, but they diverge over whether the story is chiefly about aggressive power plays or ideological fractures within the right.
summary: Republicans on the House Budget Committee advanced a $95 billion package for the Iran war , farm aid and voter ID requirements.
The resolution calls for $13 billion for Intelligence, $12 billion for Agriculture, and $10 billion for Administration for voting and elections.
The package remains a long-shot effort, too meager for some and too costly for others, ahead of voting in the full House expected next week.
title: House Republicans' $95 Billion Iran War Package Clears First Hurdle

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the meeting as a survivor-centered rebuke, leaning on charged terms like “condescending,” “gaslighting,” “damning,” and “deflating” to portray Blanche as defensive and politically wounded, while right-leaning coverage narrows the story to Tillis “stalls” on confirmation support, casting him as the key obstacle and de-emphasizing survivor reaction.
Center-Leaning pieces sit between them, stressing procedural context—who pressured whom, and why—without the sharper emotional edge.
The dividing line is whether the Epstein encounter is a moral/accountability story or a confirmation-power struggle.
Both sides agree Tillis’s vote matters, but they diverge over whether the meeting signals survivor harm or Senate leverage.
summary: On Thursday, Acting Attorney General Todd Blanche met with sex-trafficking survivors of Jeffrey Epstein at the Justice Department for over an hour, following pressure from Sen. Thom Tillis regarding the department's handling of Epstein's files.

Blanche defended the Justice Department's release of roughly 3 million files on Wednesday, acknowledging that approximately 1% of redactions contained errors and stating he takes responsibility for the mistakes.
Although Blanche declared the nearly $1.8 billion anti-weaponization fund 'dead,' Republican Sen. John Cornyn told CNN on Thursday that he believes the proposal 'still can be revived.'
Sen. Thom Tillis told CNN he requires 'definable, ratified, executed agreements' rather than verbal assurances to ensure the funding proposal is permanently discarded.

Former pardon attorney Oyer testified about her 2011 termination, which she linked to pressure regarding actor Mel Gibson's gun rights, reflecting ongoing tension between legislative demands and personnel independence at the Justice Department.
title: Blanche to meet with Epstein survivors after Tillis ultimatum

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the Maine shooting as a failure of ICE and DHS vetting, stressing “violent behavior,” “never should have been given a badge,” and even “ immigration crackdown” to widen blame beyond the individual.

Right-Leaning coverage shifts toward law-and-order and institutional response, emphasizing “federal cooperation,” “investigation,” and political reactions, while also spotlighting media conflict through phrases like “cold-blooded murder.” Both sides center the officer’s Army-veteran background and the fatal outcome, but they diverge on meaning: systemic negligence and enforcement abuse versus public-safety scrutiny and partisan overreach.
summary: On Monday, ICE officer David Brouillette shot and killed 25-year-old Colombian national Johan Sebastián Durán Guerrero while Guerrero sat in his car near his Biddeford home. DHS stated the vehicle attempted to flee and an officer discharged his weapon fearing for public safety, though Guerrero was not the intended target.

Brouillette's hiring occurred amid ICE's unprecedented expansion, adding 12,000 officers since January as part of the Trump administration's immigration crackdown. He told ex-wife Ashley late last year he had been hired by ICE; she doubted him given his long history of psychiatric issues.

Augusta District Court records document years of physical and verbal abuse allegations from his second ex-wife, including incidents where he tackled his teenage daughter and dragged her around the house. A 2021 temporary protective order cited PTSD and depression.

ICE spokesperson Lauren Bis defended the officer's qualifications, stating he has "nearly a decade of federal law enforcement experience," while the Portland Regional Chamber of Commerce called immigrants "valued members of our community." Biddobanh restaurant raised over $490,000 for Guerrero's family by Thursday.

An Associated Press investigation found applicants with questionable histories were either not fully vetted or hired despite their past. Homeland Security Secretary Markwayne Mullin announced training would increase from 42 days to 72 days starting this month following cutting-corner allegations, though the DHS inspector general's audit findings remain unreleased.
title: ICE officer in Maine shooting has history of violent behavior, family and records say

bias: Left-Leaning coverage is de-emphasized here, while the right frames the Senate move as a hard moral verdict, using loaded phrases like “should never get a pardon,” “under no circumstances,” and “rebuke” to stress punishment over process.
The center is more contextual, noting the 25-year fraud sentence, the nonbinding nature of the resolution, bipartisan sponsorship, and Trump’s broader crypto clemency pattern.

The key dividing line is whether the story is mainly a symbolic bipartisan check on clemency or a decisive condemnation of Bankman-Fried; both sides agree he should not be pardoned, but they weight accountability, legal context, and political signaling differently.
summary: On Wednesday, the U.S. Senate unanimously passed S. Res. 772, declaring that FTX founder Sam Bankman-Fried should "under no circumstances" receive executive clemency, including a pardon or commutation.
Bankman-Fried submitted a formal pardon application in early June while serving a 25-year prison sentence imposed in March 2024 for seven fraud and conspiracy charges tied to FTX's collapse.
Senators Cynthia Lummis, R-Wyo, and Ruben Gallego, D-Ariz, introduced the measure, with Lummis stating Bankman-Fried "had his day in court" and Gallego calling him a criminal deserving imprisonment.

The Senate's action follows President Donald Trump's previous clemency grants to crypto figures Ross Ulbricht in January 2025 and Changpeng Zhao in October 2025, placing the chamber on record against similar relief.

Although the resolution cannot block the president's constitutional pardon power, federal records indicate Bankman-Fried is not eligible for release until 2044, despite ongoing efforts to challenge his conviction.
title: Senate passes resolution saying Bankman-Fried shouldn’t receive pardon

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the case as a protective crackdown on tech-enabled abuse, stressing terms like “exploiting,” “sexualise minors,” and “safeguards” to spotlight deliberate harm and xAI’s reputational/legal exposure; right-leaning coverage shifts toward scandal and precedent, using phrases like “sexually explicit deepfakes ,” “terms of service,” and “one of the first” to foreground moral outrage and the broader AI-litigation implications.
The key dividing lines are the alleged bypassing of safeguards and the focus on minors—central to the left, but softened into contractual breach and novelty on the right.
Both sides agree on the lawsuit, but differ on whether it is chiefly about child exploitation or AI governance.
summary: On Tuesday, Elon Musk 's artificial-intelligence startup xAI sued South Carolina resident Terry Harwood in federal court in Texas, alleging he misused the system Grok to create child sexual abuse material.

Harwood, previously arrested on charges of sexually exploiting minors, allegedly used Grok to generate sexually explicit deepfakes and non-consensual imagery of adults, violating the company's terms of service.

Plaintiff stated the lawsuit addresses a "calculated scheme to weaponize Plaintiff's tool for criminal ends," while the company suspended 52,222 accounts and made 73,604 reports to the National Center for Missing & Exploited Children in 2026.

This case marks one of the first times an AI company has sued a user, reflecting a broader industry challenge balancing rapid tool development with safeguards against privacy violations and digital harm.

Tech companies face increasing pressure to strengthen safeguards against abuse of generative systems, with experts suggesting the outcome will influence future user accountability standards across the industry.
title: Musk’s xAI sues man accused of using Grok to create explicit material

bias: Left-Leaning coverage tends to frame Fetterman’s remarks as a Democratic-family rift, stressing reaction and skepticism with terms like “threatens,” “slam,” and “point-blank,” and often treating the “anti- Israel ” line as a litmus test amid progressive infighting; right-leaning coverage, by contrast, turns the same comments into evidence of party unraveling, using sharper phrases like “ultimate dealbreaker,” “ditch Democrats,” “red lines,” and “radicalization” to imply Democrats are pushing him toward a break.
Both sides spotlight his unusual strength with Republican voters in the poll, but the right presents it as a looming realignment while the left treats it more as a political curiosity.
summary: Fetterman has alienated some Democrats by criticizing the party's progressive wing and maintaining staunch support for Israel, while launching a joint fundraising committee with Republican Dave McCormick to boost campaign finances.

On Sunday, Representative Chris Deluzio, a Democrat, called the joint committee "crazy stuff," arguing it harms party efforts; Fetterman defended it as "nothing sinister" and based on shared interests.
In May, Fetterman told The New York Times he has "no plans" to switch parties, citing his alignment with Democrats on reproductive and LGBTQ+ rights despite pressure from conservatives.
title: Fetterman warns he would leave Democratic Party if it turns its back on Israel

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the episode as a moral insult story, using charged terms like “vile slur,” “blowing off,” and “lost their minds” to portray Vance as dismissive and the remark as a serious affront to Michelle Obama ; right-leaning coverage instead centers Vance’s “totally disproportionate” line, casting the backlash as overreaction and leaning on “false and offensive” to recast the controversy as mischaracterized.

The key dividing line is whether the event is treated as a shocking attack or a post-fight “joke” from an “amped up fighter.” Both sides agree on the core facts, but they diverge sharply on whether outrage signals accountability or partisan excess.
summary: Vice President JD Vance said the backlash to a false and offensive comment about Michelle Obama at a UFC event was totally disproportionate to the comment itself.
Vance discussed this during an episode of 'The Joe Rogan Experience.'
He noted that people say crazy things often in politics and that the worst reaction should be acknowledging the offense and moving on.
Vance expressed that many people lost their minds over the comment despite its offensiveness.
title: Vance: Reaction to Michelle Obama insult at White House UFC event ‘totally disproportionate’

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the story as self-dealing, leaning on charged phrases like “huge red flags” and “stock-gobbling” to portray Trump’s posts as a pattern of profiteering, especially with the detailed Nvidia example and the $200,000–$500,000 purchase range followed by talk of expedited permits.
Center-Leaning coverage stays more report-like, stressing that he bought shares in 20+ companies and then promoted them to millions, while also including the White House denial.

The right-leaning angle is more cautious, using “conflict of interest controversy” and noting misconduct “cannot be proven so far.” All sides agree on the core timeline, but diverge over whether it signals scandal, allegation, or unresolved concern.
summary: A CNN investigation found that President Donald Trump promoted more than 20 companies on his Truth Social account days after his managers purchased their stock, at times announcing government actions that could benefit those specific firms.

Unlike past presidents who utilized blind trusts to avoid conflicts of interest, Trump has not placed his assets in such accounts, meaning he can identify the specific stocks his financial managers buy or sell.

Trump bought between $200,000 and $500,000 in Nvidia stock days before promising to expedite permits for its AI supercomputers; he later urged Americans to "go out and buy a Dell computer" after purchasing shares in the company.

Government watchdogs labeled the trading activity a "case study in presidential conflicts of interest," though White House spokesperson Anna Kelly denied any impropriety, asserting the president "only acts in the best interests of the American public."

While Congress debates legislation to ban stock trading by lawmakers, members have not extended these restrictions to the executive branch, leaving the president exempt from the conflict-of-interest laws that bind other federal employees.
title: Trump Promoted Companies on Truth Social Days After Buying Their Stocks: CNN Report

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the 25% tariff as “unjustifiable” and even “politically motivated,” stressing possible interference in Brazil and sympathy for Lula’s warning of retaliation; right-leaning coverage instead spotlights toughness, using words like “slaps,” “socialist leader,” and “failed to negotiate in good faith” to cast the move as a justified response to bad-faith bargaining.
The sharpest divide is over Rubio’s remarks and the trade-war frame: the left treats them as hostile escalation, while the right treats them as proof Brazil forced Washington’s hand.
Both sides note the trade surplus and reciprocal tariffs , but they interpret the same numbers through starkly different ideological and geopolitical lenses.
summary: Late Wednesday, the Trump Administration announced a 25% tariff on most Brazilian imports, effective July 22, as part of a broader strategy to address unfair trade practices globally.

These tariffs follow a February Supreme Court ruling that struck down previous 50% duties, leaving only a 10% global tariff in place; U.S. Trade Representative Jamieson Greer said year-long negotiations failed to resolve trade issues.

Ahead of Brazil's October presidential election, Brazilian President Luiz Inacio Lula da Silva accused Senator Flavio Bolsonaro of contributing to the tariffs during his Washington trip; Flavio denied the claim.

Secretary of State Marco Rubio criticized the government for having 'not negotiated in good faith,' while President Lula rejected the measures, noting the U.S. recorded a $424.5 billion trade surplus with Brazil over 15 years.

Trump endorsed a revised version of the late Sen. Lindsey Graham's Russia sanctions bill, which proposes tariffs of at least 500% on countries buying Russian energy, including Brazil, India, and China.
title: Brazil Calls Trump’s 25% Tariff Unjustifiable, Vows to Impose Reciprocal Tariffs

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the crash as a safety-and-accountability story, stressing that the driver “overrode” self-driving mode and that the NTSB corroborated earlier law-enforcement findings, while center coverage offers the fullest reconstruction—driver age, street, speed limit, and the 100% accelerator press—and treats the report as confirming Tesla ’s account.

Right-Leaning coverage is much more compressed and largely reportorial, with no strong ideological language; the key dividing line is not rhetoric but emphasis on driver intervention versus system blame.
Across all sides, the shared core is the same: a fatal residential crash, an engaged assistance system, and a manual override.
summary: On Wednesday, the National Transportation Safety Board reported that the driver of a Tesla Model 3 involved in a June 19 fatal crash in Katy, Texas, manually overrode the vehicle's Full Self-Driving system, killing 76-year-old Martha Avila.

Electronic data indicates 44-year-old driver Michael Butler manually overrode the system by pressing the accelerator pedal to 100%, reaching speeds greater than 70 mph on a residential road with a 30 mph limit.

Tesla's vice president of AI software, Ashok Elluswamy, previously stated the driver manually overrode the software, and the NTSB's preliminary findings corroborate Tesla's account that driver action caused the crash.
Following the incident, Harris County law enforcement charged Butler with manslaughter, and Avila's family filed a wrongful death lawsuit against both the driver and Tesla, alleging negligence.

The NTSB plans to issue safety recommendations to prevent similar crashes, while the National Highway Traffic Safety Administration continues investigating nearly 50 Tesla incidents involving advanced driver assistance systems since 2016.
title: Fed Probe Suggests Tesla's Self-Driving Feature Wasn't to Blame in Crash that Killed a Grandmother

bias: Left-Leaning coverage turns the shutdown into a story about Ben & Jerry’s “progressive mission,” “iconic” brand identity, and even claims Magnum is trying to “destroy” its social values, making the funding cut feel like an ideological attack; right-leaning coverage strips that away into a terse, event-first frame, emphasizing “shut down,” “legal dispute,” and “parent company” with little activist context.
The pivotal divide is whether the case is a corporate housekeeping issue or a cultural fight over mission and ownership.
Both sides agree on the $600,000 grants, the funding cut, and the eviction, but they differ sharply on whether those facts signal routine litigation or a broader political struggle.
summary: On Tuesday, the Ben & Jerry's Foundation announced it will suspend operations on December 31, 2026, unless it wins a legal challenge against its parent company, The Magnum Ice Cream Company.
Following an audit revealing governance and financial control issues, Magnum—a Unilever spinoff—withheld funding and ordered the foundation to vacate its South Burlington offices by July 15.

According to Board of Trustees president Liz Bankowski, the dispute jeopardizes approximately $600,000 in annual grants for Vermont organizations and threatens the foundation's 40-year mission of supporting progressive social change.

Co-Founder Ben Cohen launched a petition amassing around 160,000 signatures demanding Magnum sell the brand, while the foundation pursues litigation in the U.S. District Court for the Southern District of New York.
Foundation leaders characterize the dispute as a "David and Goliath" fight, vowing to continue their battle for independence as legal proceedings are expected to extend into next year.
title: Ben & Jerry’s Foundation says it will shut down amid legal dispute with parent company

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the story as “Trump’s term” extending an ongoing political act, stressing duration and implied controversy, sometimes with local context like Mayor Muriel Bowser; the right-leaning side instead casts it as a security measure, using phrases like “crime emergency,” “stop crime,” and “remain deployed” to justify the Guard’s presence.

The biggest dividing lines are purpose and scale: the right highlights the original executive order and “5,000” troops, while the left de-emphasizes that rationale and focuses on the extension itself.
Both sides agree on the Pentagon ’s confirmation and the 2029 endpoint, but they reflect deeper splits over law-and-order versus institutional overreach.
summary: The Pentagon confirmed Thursday the National Guard deployment in Washington will continue through Jan. 20, 2029, extending President Donald Trump's mission until the end of his term.
Trump issued an executive order in August 2025 declaring a crime emergency, deploying troops to the capital; the mission was previously set to expire at year's end.
Council Chairman Phil Mendelson and city officials have opposed the deployment as unnecessary and costly, noting taxpayers pay more than a million dollars daily for the presence.
On July 9, The Council sent letters to Michigan Gov. Gretchen Whitmer and Virgin Islands Gov. Albert Bryan requesting withdrawal of their National Guard soldiers from the city.

Because Washington is a congressionally established federal district, Trump maintains direct control over the Guard, allowing him to sidestep legal challenges that halted similar deployments in California and Illinois.
title: National Guard Deployment to DC Will Last Through Trump’s Term

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the case as ethical wrongdoing, using phrases like “violating ethics” and foregrounding the inmate’s “excruciating pain” to stress harm and accountability; the center intensifies that critique further with “botched execution,” “malpractice,” and even “torture,” turning the story into an explicit indictment of institutional failure.
The right-leaning piece, however, is not less critical here—it also centers the complaint and “excruciating pain,” while emphasizing institutional misconduct and controversy.
All three agree the execution was halted by IV problems and that a health-department complaint was filed, but they diverge on how sharply they moralize the state’s role.
summary: Tonya Hervey filed an ethics complaint Wednesday with the Tennessee Department of Health regarding Mark Fowler's role in the May 21 execution attempt of her brother, Tony Carruthers, who she alleges suffered partial paralysis.

Following the botched execution attempt, Gov Bill Lee halted Carruthers' execution and granted a one-year reprieve; Carruthers was sentenced for the 1994 murders of Marcellos Anderson, Delois Anderson, and Frederick Tucker.

Fowler allegedly violated medical ethics by injecting Carruthers with lidocaine without allergy testing and continuing to search for a vein despite his "visible agony and distress," while also claiming the doctor was unqualified.

Regarding the allegations, the Tennessee Department of Health stated complaint information remains confidential until formal charges are filed, while the Tennessee Department of Correction and Fowler both declined to comment.

Ervin Yen, a retired cardiac anesthesiologist who has witnessed Oklahoma executions, noted that the doctor's lack of recent central line experience is not necessarily disqualifying, though he deemed a stroke from IV attempts "very unlikely.
title: Complaint accuses doctor involved in failed Tennessee execution of violating ethics

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the story as Trump’s frustrated reaction to networks declining to amplify his claims, stressing “lies,” “blather,” and even “humiliation” to portray the speech as dubious and his license-threat as overreach.

Right-Leaning coverage, by contrast, casts the same decision as media “avoidance” or “censorship,” using charged words like “slams,” “skip,” “corrupt,” and “bombshell” to suggest broadcasters were suppressing major election-integrity revelations.

The key dividing line is whether the refusal to air is editorial caution or partisan obstruction; both sides agree the networks passed on live coverage and Trump threatened licenses, but they split sharply over whether that signals responsible skepticism or anti-Trump bias.
summary: On Thursday, July 16, 2026, major U.S. broadcast networks including ABC and NBC declined to air President Donald Trump's prime-time address live, instead routing coverage to streaming platforms and analysis to address potential misinformation.

Broadcasters cited concerns about amplifying unverified claims regarding the 2020 election, following established industry precedents where networks previously limited coverage of President Barack Obama's 2014 immigration address.

Federal Communications Commission chair Brendan Carr has initiated reviews into broadcast station licenses, creating regulatory pressure that complicates editorial independence during the election cycle.

During his address, Trump threatened to revoke broadcast licenses and accused networks of bias, while CNN integrated real-time fact-checking with a breaking news banner stating Trump gives address on elections after years of false claims.
Editorial standards remain a central focus as media organizations navigate Trump's efforts to exert control over coverage throughout the 2026 election cycle, testing longstanding journalistic norms.
title: NBC, ABC, CNN won’t carry Trump speech on networks

bias: Left coverage frames the outbreak as an urgent public-health crisis, using alarmed terms like “explosive diarrhea,” “urgent warning,” and even “guilty lettuce,” while right coverage intensifies the drama with words like “tainted,” “JUST IN,” and “diarrhea debacle,” often presenting investigators as “zeroing in” on a culprit.

The dividing line is whether Taco Bell ’s lettuce removal is portrayed as a necessary precaution or as evidence of contamination, with both sides spotlighting the supplier and the thousands of cases, but the right more often widens the scare across “34 states” and the left stresses immediate consumer risk.
Both converge on the outbreak’s seriousness and Michigan’s heavy impact, yet diverge sharply on tone, certainty, and scale.
summary: On Thursday, the Food and Drug Administration launched an investigation into Taylor Farms shredded lettuce supplied to Taco Bell Corp restaurants, citing a potential link to a multistate cyclosporiasis outbreak.
The parasitic illness causes weeks of severe, watery diarrhea, with the current outbreak centered in the Midwest, where Michigan alone reports more than 4,300 cases.
A Taco Bell spokesperson said Tuesday the company "voluntarily and temporarily removed limited ingredients" as a precaution, though officials have not confirmed a specific link to the restaurant.
Taylor Farms has been connected to previous foodborne illness outbreaks, including a 2013 salad mix incident and a 2024 E. coli outbreak tied to McDonald's Quarter Pounder hamburgers.
Nationwide, nearly 7,000 cases of cyclosporiasis are confirmed or under investigation since May 1, though identifying sources remains difficult due to the parasite's two-week incubation period.
title: Taco Bell removes lettuce in some states over cyclosporiasis link

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the call as a contested hold on territory, stressing “security zones” in quotes, “pretext,” and skepticism toward Israel ’s rationale, while right-leaning coverage recasts the same move as protection, leaning on “security talks,” “protect,” and the emotionally charged “October 7 massacre” to justify troop retention.

The sharpest dividing line is whether the zones signify occupation or defense; left outlets foreground that tension, while right outlets broaden the story into Iran, regional escalation, and border safety.
Both sides agree on Katz and Hegseth speaking and troops staying put, but they diverge over whether that posture signals militarized control or necessary deterrence.
summary: Early Thursday, Israeli Defense Minister Israel Katz told U.S. Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth that Israel is determined to maintain its "security zones" inside Lebanon, Syria, and the Gaza Strip to protect its borders from jihadist threats.
Reports from Axios suggest President Donald Trump recently urged Israel to withdraw forces from these buffer zones, though neither Washington nor Jerusalem has publicly confirmed the request.

Israel currently controls 60 percent of the Gaza Strip and maintains a "security zone" extending roughly 10 kilometres into Lebanon, which officials claim prevents terrorist groups from re-establishing positions near Israeli communities.
Hegseth updated Katz on U.S. strikes against Iranian infrastructure as regional tensions escalate, with Iran launching missiles at U.S. facilities in Saudi Arabia, Bahrain, Kuwait, and Jordan.
Negotiations concluded a fifth round in Rome on Wednesday, with U.S.-brokered talks aiming to establish "pilot zones" to facilitate a steady withdrawal of Israeli forces from southern Lebanon.
title: Israel tells Pentagon chief will keep troops in Lebanon, Syria, Gaza 'security zones'

bias: Left and center coverage foreground the UN official’s accusation and the humanitarian risk, using restrained terms like “obstruction,” “interfering,” and “intimidating aid workers,” while the right sharpens the story into a security threat, adding “terrorists,” “storm,” and “seizing humanitarian assistance.” The biggest dividing line is specificity: the right highlights the Jabalia food-distribution point, a WFP warehouse, and COGAT’s blame, turning a general aid dispute into a concrete, militarized incident; the left de-emphasizes those details.
Still, all sides converge on Hamas blocking aid and endangering relief operations, reflecting a shared concern split by broader ideological views on culpability and conflict.
summary: On Monday, UN Deputy Special Coordinator for the Middle East Peace Process Ramiz Alakbarov accused Hamas of obstructing humanitarian operations, warning the group's interference makes relief efforts 'increasingly dangerous' for aid workers.

Armed men linked to Hamas allegedly forced their way into a Jabalia food distribution point on Saturday, reportedly assaulting two World Food Programme truck drivers and entering a warehouse, per a United Nations statement.

Alakbarov stated these incidents are 'not isolated,' warning they 'reflect an increasingly dangerous pattern of intimidation, violence and obstruction, including smuggling attempts, targeting and abusing humanitarian operations' affecting Gaza civilians.

An official from the Hamas-run interior ministry rejected the accusations as unfounded, while COGAT, the Israeli defence body, slammed the group for allegedly 'cynically exploiting the humanitarian space' intended for Gaza residents.
Israeli forces have expanded their presence across more than 60% of Gaza territory in recent months, though Hamas continues to exert control over the remaining areas amid ongoing tensions.
title: Top UN official accuses Hamas of Gaza aid obstruction

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames Abu Safiya as a humanitarian victim of deliberate, possibly lethal mistreatment, using charged terms like “imminent danger,” “deliberate,” and “scandalous,” and often broadening the story into attacks on doctors, hospitals, and calls for sanctions.
Right-Leaning coverage pivots toward security suspicion, with phrases like “Hamas colonel” and hints of “systematic targeting” or militant imagery, treating the case through a counterterror lens.
Center coverage stays more restrained, stressing “arbitrary” detention and UN/legal findings.
All sides agree he is detained and that UN bodies want his release, but ideology divides the interpretation of why.
summary: On Wednesday, a United Nations inquiry labeled the 18-month detention of Dr. Hussam Abu Safiya, director of Gaza's Kamal Adwan Hospital, arbitrary and urged his immediate release, citing violations of international human rights instruments.

Israeli forces seized Abu Safiya in December 2024, alleging the pediatrician was a Hamas member, though rights groups note authorities have produced no public evidence supporting these combatant claims during his detention without charge.

Lawyer Nasser Odeh reported after a July 2 prison visit that the doctor is nearly unrecognizable, suffering severe injuries to his 'head, eyes, ears, and neck' while struggling to breathe in the Rakefet interrogation facility.
Multiple UK Members of Parliament, including Jeremy Corbyn, former Labour leader, and Nadia Whittome, Labour MP, have urged the government to use diplomatic leverage to secure Abu Safiya's release.

The UN inquiry warned this case 'may indicate a widespread or systematic practice' of arbitrary detention, while UN Special Rapporteur Francesca Albanese criticized the BBC for presenting unproven Israeli military claims regarding the doctor.
title: UN inquiry urges release of Gaza doctor held by Israel since December 2024

bias: Left and center coverage frame the story as a restrained, procedural “probe” into a “possible” violation, emphasizing uncertainty and a request for DOJ review, while right-leaning outlets sharpen it into a law-and-order drama with terms like “Exclusive,” “Potential Logan Act Violation,” and “national security risks.” The right also adds partisan color through “GOP” and ideological labeling like “Democrat socialist,” turning the meeting with Iranian officials into a suspected threat, not just an inquiry.
A key dividing line is whether the focus is on investigation or culpability, though all sides agree House Republicans want DOJ action over the Iran -linked meeting.
summary: House Republicans led by Rep. Addison McDowell requested the Department of Justice investigate potential Logan Act violations by New York City Mayor Zohran Mamdani 's administration regarding unauthorized diplomacy with Iran.
Commissioner Ana María Archila of the Mayor's Office for International Affairs arranged a July 7 meeting with Iran's U.N. ambassador, which was canceled after federal officials intervened.
The State Department condemned the planned meeting as inappropriate during sensitive U.S.-Iran negotiations and reprimanded Archila while clarifying acceptable conduct.
Mayor Mamdani stated the meeting invitation originated externally and not from his office, and his administration is updating protocols for such meeting requests.
title: House Republicans Request DOJ Investigation Into Mamdani Following Iran Meeting Report

bias: Left-Leaning coverage is de-emphasized here, while the center frames Tim Scott ’s preference for Trey Gowdy in a restrained, single-track way; the right expands the story into a more energized GOP succession drama, using phrases like “BIG UPDATE,” “heats up,” and “popular Fox News host” to make the contest feel urgent and ideologically familiar.

One side spotlights Scott’s endorsement and Gowdy’s prosecutorial and foreign-policy credentials; the other broadens into speculation about a “conservative figure” and even notes Darline Graham Nordone’s swearing-in.
Across both, the shared focus is the Aug. 11 special election, but the divide is between pragmatic seat-filling and a larger conservative-media-inflected political opening.
summary: Senator Tim Scott supports former Representative Trey Gowdy as a strong candidate to succeed the late Senator Lindsey Graham and praises Gowdy's experience and expertise in law and foreign policy.
Trey Gowdy retired from Congress in 2019, now hosts a Fox News show, and has expressed disinterest in running for the Senate seat despite encouragement from Scott.
Senator Darline Graham, the late Senator Lindsey Graham's sister, was appointed to complete his Senate term and is regarded by Scott as a capable interim senator.
Representative Russell Fry is being considered as a potential candidate for the Senate seat, with no official White House endorsement made yet.
title: Tim Scott Says He Would Like Trey Gowdy to Succeed Lindsey Graham in the Senate

bias: Left-Leaning coverage centers on Greer’s dismissal, framing Canada’s moves as having “meant little” and echoing phrases like “don’t really get credit” and “something bad and then undoing it,” which casts the concessions as politically unrewarded and morally loaded.

Right-Leaning coverage shifts from that quotation to the broader impasse, using tougher, more adversarial language like “pressure” and “remain stalled” to portray negotiations as stuck despite concessions.

The key dividing line is whether the story is about Trump’s refusal to reward Canada or about a wider trade deadlock; both sides agree the concessions and talks are connected, but they emphasize different stakes.
summary: The U.S. Trade Representative Jamieson Greer stated the Trump administration will not give Canada credit for its trade concessions, including dropping the digital services tax and rolling back the Online Streaming Act.
Prime Minister Mark Carney took actions aimed at advancing trade talks with the United States.
Washington and Mexico have started formal negotiations on the Canada-U.S.-Mexico trade agreement, but no such talks have begun with Canada.
Greer stated he is in weekly contact with Canadian officials and has offered proposals to improve relations, hoping a deal between President Trump and Carney could overcome barriers.
title: Canada’s Concessions Have Meant Little to Trump Administration: U.S. Trade Czar
